text: The surgeon uncovers an abscess near the left pelvic side wall, complicating the dissection. The bowel is scored at the distal extent, marking a potential site for anastomosis. The surgeon performs a lateral to medial dissection, encountering dense adhesions along the left pelvic side wall.

The surgeon attempts to mobilize the left dome of the bladder, encountering dense adhesions. Traction is applied to gain mobility, leading to the inadvertent opening of a chronic abscess cavity, revealing the source of the adhesions.

The surgeon performs a leak test through the rectum to confirm there is no perforation into the bowel. Unable to find a suitable approach to the area, the surgeon decides to proceed through the mesentery, utilizing an inadvertently created window for increased mobility.

The surgeon works down to the upper rectum, selecting the margin on the left side of the rectum. The mesentery is separated from the sidewall, revealing a clearer view of the area. The surgeon carefully dissects through the abscess cavity, ensuring not to inadvertently pull the ureter or iliac vessels from the sidewall.

The surgeon skives into the rectal mesentery, revealing a clear separation of the upper rectum from the surrounding cavity. The proximal bowel is stapled, establishing the proximal margin, which is visibly far from the initial scoring on the serosal surface of the bowel.
The surgeon staples the distal bowel and begins the sewn anastomosis using a PDS suture, focusing on the back row of the anastomosis.

The surgeon sews the second inner layer of the anastomosis, with the suture moving towards the patient's right side. The specimen is extracted through a Pfannenstiel incision, which is visible with minimal tissue disruption.

The surgeon performs the outer row of Tuo PDS sutures to imbricate the anastomosis layer, ensuring it remains widely patent without narrowing. The sutures are placed meticulously, taking longer than using a stapler but providing secure closure.
